skill.name: find-bugs
skill.description: Guides code review of current branch changes to find bugs, security vulnerabilities, and code quality issues before merge.
skill.summary: Review branch diffs for bugs, vulnerabilities, and code quality risks.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: The static external command findings are confirmed as security-relevant because the skill instructs use of git and GitHub CLI commands. The hardcoded URL and weak cryptography detections are false positives in metadata and prose. No prompt injection, malicious intent, credential theft, or file modification behavior was found.

medium_findings.title: External Command Workflow Requires User Trust
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs the assistant to run a git diff command that includes GitHub CLI command substitution. This is legitimate for reviewing branch changes, but it executes local commands and may contact GitHub.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command is directly present and is central to the skill workflow. I found no evidence that it writes files, reads secrets, or sends repository contents to an unauthorized service.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded Source URL Is Metadata
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URL appears in the skill frontmatter as a source reference. It is not an instruction to make an external request by itself, so the static network finding is a false positive.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line 4 is clearly labeled as source metadata. No runtime behavior or data transfer is attached to that URL in the skill text.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Detections Are Prose Matches
low_findings.description: The static weak cryptography findings point to general security review language and output format text. No weak cryptographic algorithm or cryptographic implementation is present.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines contain description and severity-label prose, not code or algorithm choices. I found no evidence of MD5, SHA1, DES, ECB mode, or similar weak cryptographic use.

dangerous_patterns.title: Shell Command Substitution in Review Command
dangerous_patterns.description: The Markdown command example uses command substitution to obtain the default branch before running git diff. This pattern should be treated as command execution even though it is expected for this skill.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command substitution syntax is visible on the cited line. The risk is bounded because the command is a review helper and there is no instruction to modify files or exfiltrate secrets.

content.value_statement: Branch changes can hide bugs, security gaps, and missing tests. This skill guides Claude, Codex, or Claude Code through a structured review of local diffs.
actual_capabilities: Guides review of the full branch diff against the default branch.
Maps user inputs, database queries, authorization checks, and external calls.
Applies a security checklist for injection, XSS, CSRF, sessions, and denial of service.
Prompts verification through surrounding code and existing tests.
Formats findings with severity, evidence, concrete fixes, and references.
limitations: Requires access to the repository and Git history for complete diff review.
May use GitHub CLI, which can require authentication and network access.
Does not automatically fix issues or edit files.
Cannot prove absence of bugs when tests, context, or runtime behavior are incomplete.

use_cases.title: Pre-Merge Security Review
use_cases.description: Review a feature branch before merge and identify exploitable changes with concrete evidence.

use_cases.title: Pull Request Bug Sweep
use_cases.description: Check changed files for logic errors, missing edge cases, and test gaps before reviewer handoff.

use_cases.title: Release Risk Assessment
use_cases.description: Audit release branch changes for authentication, authorization, data handling, and stability risks.

prompt_templates.title: Review My Branch
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner review of current branch changes.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use find-bugs to review my current branch changes. Focus on real bugs and security issues, then report only findings with evidence.

prompt_templates.title: Check a Pull Request
prompt_templates.scenario: Standard pull request review before merge.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use find-bugs on this branch. Compare it with the default branch, read every changed file, and list verified issues by severity.

prompt_templates.title: Security-Focused Audit
prompt_templates.scenario: Focused review for application security risks.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use find-bugs for a security review. Prioritize injection, authorization, CSRF, session handling, information disclosure, and denial of service.

prompt_templates.title: Deep Release Review
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced review of high-risk release changes.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use find-bugs for a deep release audit. Map the attack surface, verify each finding against tests and context, and include standards references when useful.

output_examples.input: Review my authentication branch for security issues.
output: High: Password reset endpoint accepts tokens without checking expiration.
Evidence: The changed handler validates token existence but not expiry, and no test covers expired tokens.
Fix: Reject expired tokens before account updates and add a regression test.

output_examples.input: Find bugs in this checkout flow change.
output: Medium: Cart totals can be recalculated after payment intent creation.
Evidence: The updated flow reads mutable cart state after authorization.
Fix: Store an immutable order snapshot before payment confirmation.

output_examples.input: Audit this pull request and tell me if it is clean.
output_examples.output: No significant security vulnerabilities or correctness bugs were found in the reviewed changes. The report lists reviewed files and areas that could not be fully verified.

best_practices: Run the review from a clean working tree so the diff is accurate.
Allow the assistant to read full changed files before accepting findings.
Ask for verification against tests and surrounding code for each issue.
anti_patterns: Do not use the skill for style-only reviews or formatting feedback.
Do not accept speculative findings without file, line, evidence, and impact.
Do not run GitHub CLI commands if repository network access is not allowed.

faq.question: Does this skill change my code?
faq.answer: No. The skill instructs the assistant to report findings and avoid making changes.

faq.question: Why does it use Git commands?
faq.answer: It needs the branch diff to identify changed files and review only relevant code.

faq.question: Does it require GitHub access?
faq.answer: It may use GitHub CLI to discover the default branch. Local alternatives can be used if network access is restricted.

faq.question: Can it find every vulnerability?
faq.answer: No. It improves review coverage, but incomplete context or missing runtime data can hide issues.

faq.question: What findings should I expect?
faq.answer: Expect prioritized bugs, security vulnerabilities, evidence, suggested fixes, and references when applicable.

faq.question: Can Claude Code and Codex use it?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill is written for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code workflows that can inspect local repositories.
